title: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
content: CPR is a life-saving technique used when someone's heart stops beating. Steps: 1) Check responsiveness - tap shoulders and shout 'Are you okay?'. 2) Call for help or ask someone to call emergency services. 3) Place the person on their back on a firm surface. 4) Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (between the nipples), place your other hand on top. 5) Push hard and fast - compress at least 2 inches deep at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute. 6) After 30 compressions, tilt the head back, lift the chin, and give 2 rescue breaths. 7) Continue the cycle of 30 compressions and 2 breaths. 8) Do not stop until help arrives or the person starts breathing. For infants, use 2 fingers instead of hands. For children, use one hand. WARNING: Do not perform CPR on someone who is breathing normally.

title: Treating Burns
content: Burns are classified by severity: First-degree burns affect only the outer layer of skin (redness, minor swelling). Second-degree burns affect deeper layers (blisters, severe pain). Third-degree burns destroy all layers (white/charred skin, may be painless due to nerve damage). Treatment for minor burns (first and second degree): 1) Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water for at least 10-20 minutes. 2) Do NOT apply ice, butter, or toothpaste. 3) Remove rings, watches, or tight clothing near the burn before swelling starts. 4) Apply aloe vera gel or a moisturizing lotion. 5) Cover loosely with a sterile non-stick bandage. 6) Take over-the-counter pain relievers if needed. For severe burns (third degree): 1) Do NOT remove burned clothing stuck to the skin. 2) Cover with a clean, cool, moist cloth. 3) Elevate the burned area above heart level if possible. 4) Watch for signs of shock. 5) Seek emergency medical care immediately. WARNING: Never pop blisters as this increases infection risk.

title: Snake Bite First Aid
content: Snake bites can be life-threatening. Immediate actions: 1) Move away from the snake - do not try to catch or kill it. 2) Keep the victim calm and still - movement spreads venom faster. 3) Remove jewelry, watches, and tight clothing near the bite. 4) Position the bitten limb at or below heart level. 5) Clean the wound gently with soap and water if available. 6) Apply a pressure immobilization bandage: wrap a bandage firmly (not too tight) starting from the bite site toward the heart. 7) Immobilize the limb with a splint. 8) Mark the edge of swelling with a pen and note the time. 9) Transport to medical facility as quickly as possible. CRITICAL: Do NOT cut the wound, do NOT try to suck out venom, do NOT apply a tourniquet, do NOT apply ice. Try to remember the snake's appearance (color, size, head shape) to help identify it. Common venomous snake symptoms include: severe pain, swelling, nausea, difficulty breathing, blurred vision, and numbness.

title: Wound Care and Bleeding Control
content: Proper wound care prevents infection and promotes healing. For minor wounds: 1) Wash hands before treating the wound. 2) Stop bleeding by applying gentle pressure with a clean cloth for 5-10 minutes. 3) Clean the wound under running water. Remove debris with sterilized tweezers. 4) Apply antibiotic ointment if available. 5) Cover with a sterile bandage. 6) Change the bandage daily. For severe bleeding: 1) Apply direct, firm pressure with a clean cloth or bandage. 2) If blood soaks through, add more layers - do NOT remove the first cloth. 3) Elevate the injured area above the heart if possible. 4) For life-threatening limb bleeding, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound. Note the time. 5) Keep the victim warm to prevent shock. Signs of infection to watch for: increasing pain, redness spreading from wound, swelling, warmth, pus or drainage, fever, red streaks from wound. WARNING: Deep puncture wounds, animal bites, and wounds with embedded objects require professional medical attention.

title: Dehydration Recognition and Treatment
content: Dehydration occurs when the body loses more fluid than it takes in. Symptoms by severity - Mild: thirst, dry mouth, dark yellow urine, headache. Moderate: very dry mouth, little or no urination, sunken eyes, dizziness, rapid heartbeat. Severe: extreme thirst, no urination, very dry skin, rapid breathing, confusion, fainting. Treatment: 1) For mild dehydration: drink small sips of water frequently rather than large amounts at once. 2) Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS) recipe: mix 6 level teaspoons of sugar and half a level teaspoon of salt in 1 liter of clean water. 3) Avoid caffeine and alcohol - these worsen dehydration. 4) Rest in a cool, shaded area. 5) If vomiting: give small frequent sips (1 teaspoon every 1-2 minutes). 6) For children: continue breastfeeding and give ORS between feeds. 7) For severe dehydration: seek medical help immediately - IV fluids may be needed. Prevention: drink water regularly, especially in hot weather or during physical activity. Adults need approximately 2-3 liters per day in normal conditions, more in heat.

title: Fever Management
content: Fever is a body temperature above 38°C (100.4°F). It is usually a sign the body is fighting infection. Management steps: 1) Rest and stay hydrated - drink plenty of water, clear broths, or oral rehydration solution. 2) Light clothing - do not bundle up, this can trap heat. 3) Cool compress - place a damp cloth on the forehead, neck, and armpits. 4) Over-the-counter medication: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) or ibuprofen can reduce fever. Follow dosage instructions. Do NOT give aspirin to children under 16. 5) Monitor temperature every 2-4 hours. 6) Tepid sponge bath can help if temperature is very high. Do NOT use cold water or alcohol rubs. When to seek emergency care: temperature above 40°C (104°F), fever lasting more than 3 days, severe headache with stiff neck, rash that doesn't fade when pressed, difficulty breathing, persistent vomiting, confusion or unusual drowsiness, seizures. For infants under 3 months, any fever above 38°C requires immediate medical attention.

title: Choking First Aid (Heimlich Maneuver)
content: Choking occurs when an object blocks the airway. Signs include: inability to speak, cough, or breathe; clutching the throat; turning blue; making high-pitched noises. For adults and children over 1 year: 1) Ask 'Are you choking?' - if they cannot speak, act immediately. 2) Stand behind the person, wrap your arms around their waist. 3) Make a fist with one hand, place it just above the navel, below the ribcage. 4) Grasp your fist with the other hand. 5) Perform quick upward thrusts - repeat until the object is expelled. 6) If the person becomes unconscious, lower them to the ground and begin CPR. For infants under 1 year: 1) Place the infant face-down on your forearm, supporting the head. 2) Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades. 3) Turn the infant face-up, give 5 chest thrusts using two fingers on the breastbone. 4) Repeat until the object is expelled. For yourself (alone): perform abdominal thrusts using a chair back or countertop edge. WARNING: Do not perform blind finger sweeps as this may push the object deeper.

title: Fracture and Sprain Management
content: Fractures (broken bones) and sprains (ligament injuries) require proper immobilization. Signs of a fracture: severe pain, swelling, bruising, deformity, inability to move the area, grinding sensation. Signs of a sprain: pain, swelling, bruising, limited motion, popping sound at injury. Treatment for suspected fractures: 1) Do NOT try to realign the bone. 2) Immobilize the area using a splint - use any rigid material (sticks, boards, rolled magazines). 3) Pad the splint for comfort. 4) Secure with bandages, cloth strips, or tape - not too tight. 5) Apply ice wrapped in cloth (15 minutes on, 15 minutes off). 6) Elevate the injured area. 7) Monitor circulation below the injury (check for numbness, tingling, blue color). For sprains, use RICE: Rest the injured area. Ice for 15-20 minutes every 2-3 hours. Compression with an elastic bandage. Elevation above heart level. WARNING: Open fractures (bone protruding through skin) require immediate emergency care. Cover the wound with a clean dressing, do not push the bone back.

title: Allergic Reaction and Anaphylaxis
content: Allergic reactions range from mild to life-threatening anaphylaxis. Mild symptoms: hives, itching, sneezing, watery eyes. Moderate: widespread hives, swelling of face or lips, abdominal pain, vomiting. Severe anaphylaxis: difficulty breathing, throat swelling, rapid pulse, dizziness, loss of consciousness. Treatment for mild reactions: 1) Remove the allergen if possible. 2) Antihistamines (diphenhydramine/cetirizine) can help. 3) Cool compresses for skin reactions. For anaphylaxis: 1) Call emergency services immediately. 2) If the person has an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen), help them use it - inject into the outer thigh. 3) Have them lie flat with legs elevated (unless they have breathing difficulty - then keep them sitting up). 4) Loosen tight clothing. 5) If breathing stops, begin CPR. 6) A second dose of epinephrine can be given after 5-15 minutes if symptoms don't improve. Common triggers: bee stings, peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish, medications (penicillin), latex. WARNING: Anaphylaxis can be fatal within minutes without treatment.

title: Heat Stroke and Heat Exhaustion
content: Heat-related illness occurs when the body cannot cool itself properly. Heat exhaustion symptoms: heavy sweating, weakness, cold and clammy skin, nausea, fast weak pulse, dizziness. Heat stroke symptoms: body temperature above 40°C (104°F), hot and dry skin (no sweating), rapid strong pulse, confusion, loss of consciousness. Heat exhaustion treatment: 1) Move to a cool place. 2) Lie down and elevate legs. 3) Apply cool wet cloths to skin. 4) Sip cool water slowly. 5) Remove excess clothing. Heat stroke treatment (MEDICAL EMERGENCY): 1) Call emergency services immediately. 2) Move to the coolest area available. 3) Cool the person rapidly - immerse in cool water if possible, or wet the skin and fan vigorously. 4) Place ice packs on neck, armpits, and groin. 5) Do NOT give fluids if unconscious. 6) Monitor breathing and prepare for CPR if needed. Prevention: drink water regularly, avoid peak sun hours (10am-4pm), wear lightweight light-colored clothing, take breaks in shade. WARNING: Heat stroke is a medical emergency. Delayed treatment can cause organ damage or death.

title: Water Purification Methods
content: Clean water is the most critical survival need. The human body can only survive about 3 days without water. Purification methods: 1) BOILING: The most reliable method. Bring water to a rolling boil for at least 1 minute (3 minutes at elevations above 2000m). Let cool before drinking. 2) SOLAR DISINFECTION (SODIS): Fill clear plastic bottles with water. Shake to oxygenate. Place in direct sunlight for at least 6 hours (2 days if cloudy). UV rays kill pathogens. 3) CHEMICAL TREATMENT: Use water purification tablets (iodine or chlorine dioxide). Follow package instructions. Usually 30 minutes wait time. 4) FILTRATION: Commercial filters are ideal. DIY filter: layer gravel, sand, and charcoal in a container with holes at bottom. Pour water through multiple times. This removes sediment but not all pathogens - combine with boiling. 5) UV PURIFICATION: SteriPEN or similar devices use UV light. Stir for 90 seconds in clear water. Water sources to look for: flowing streams (better than stagnant water), morning dew, rain collection, plant transpiration bags. WARNING: Never drink saltwater, urine, or water with chemical contamination. Always purify water from unknown sources.

title: Fire Starting Techniques
content: Fire provides warmth, light, water purification, cooking, signaling, and psychological comfort. Essential fire-starting steps: 1) GATHER MATERIALS before starting: Tinder (dry leaves, bark shavings, cotton, dryer lint, paper), Kindling (small twigs, pencil-thick sticks), Fuel (larger sticks and logs). 2) PREPARE A FIRE PIT: Clear a 3-meter diameter area. Create a ring of rocks if possible. 3) FIRE-STARTING METHODS: a) Matches/Lighter: Most reliable. Keep waterproof matches in survival kit. b) Ferro rod/Flint and steel: Strike steel against flint to create sparks onto tinder. c) Bow drill (friction method): Carve a fireboard with a notch, use a spindle and bow to create friction and heat. Requires practice. d) Hand drill: Spin a stick rapidly between palms on a fireboard. e) Magnifying lens: Focus sunlight onto tinder. Works with eyeglasses, binoculars, or clear ice. 4) BUILD THE FIRE: Start with tinder, add kindling in a teepee shape, gradually add larger fuel. Fire structures: Teepee (good for starting), Log cabin (long burning), Star (conserves fuel), Dakota hole (hidden, wind-resistant). Safety: Never leave fire unattended. Have water or dirt nearby to extinguish. Build away from overhanging branches.

title: Emergency Shelter Building
content: Shelter is a top priority in survival situations - exposure can kill faster than hunger or thirst. Key principles: protect from wind, rain, cold, heat, and ground moisture. Shelter types: 1) LEAN-TO: Simplest shelter. Find or place a long pole against a tree or between two supports. Lean branches at 45° angle along one side. Cover with leaves, bark, or debris. Build facing away from wind. 2) DEBRIS HUT: A-frame structure covered with thick layers of debris. Build frame with ridgepole and ribs. Pile leaves, grass, and debris at least 1 meter thick for insulation. Make it just big enough for your body to conserve heat. 3) SNOW CAVE: Dig into a packed snow bank. Create a sleeping platform higher than the entrance (warm air rises). Poke a ventilation hole. Smooth the ceiling to prevent dripping. 4) TARP/PONCHO SHELTER: If you have a tarp, create an A-frame using rope between two trees. Secure edges with rocks. 5) NATURAL SHELTERS: Look for caves, rock overhangs, fallen trees, dense evergreen canopies. Site selection: higher ground (avoid flooding), near water but not in a flood zone, natural windbreak, accessible materials, avoid dead trees that could fall. Insulate from the ground using leaves, boughs, or a sleeping pad - ground contact is a major heat loss factor.

title: Signaling for Rescue
content: If lost or stranded, making yourself visible to rescuers is critical. Signaling methods: 1) SIGNAL FIRE: Build three fires in a triangle (international distress signal) spaced about 30 meters apart. Add green branches or damp leaves to create thick smoke during day. At night, bright flames are more visible. 2) MIRROR/REFLECTIVE SIGNAL: Use any reflective surface (mirror, phone screen, foil, belt buckle). Aim reflected light at aircraft or distant points. Can be seen up to 50 km away. Use the 'V' sight method with two fingers. 3) WHISTLE: Three short blasts is the universal distress signal. Sound carries further than shouting and uses less energy. Carry a whistle in your survival kit. 4) GROUND SIGNALS: Create large symbols in open areas using rocks, branches, or trampled snow. Standard symbols: V = need assistance, X = need medical help, → = traveling this direction, SOS. Make symbols at least 3 meters long and high contrast with surroundings. 5) BRIGHT CLOTHING/MARKERS: Hang brightly colored clothing or gear in visible locations. 6) FLASHLIGHT/STROBE: At night, flash SOS pattern (3 short, 3 long, 3 short). Stay near your last known position if possible - rescuers will search there first.

title: Finding and Preparing Wild Food
content: Food is lower priority than water and shelter but becomes essential after 72 hours. Safe food sources: 1) INSECTS: Most nutritious and abundant. Safe options: grasshoppers, crickets, ants, larvae, earthworms. Remove wings and legs from grasshoppers. Cook when possible to kill parasites. Avoid brightly colored insects, hairy caterpillars, and insects that smell bad. 2) PLANTS: Use the Universal Edibility Test if unsure - test on skin, lips, tongue in stages over 8 hours. Safe if no reaction. Generally safe plants: dandelions (all parts), cattails (roots, shoots), pine needles (tea), acorns (leach tannins first), berries (blackberries, blueberries). AVOID: mushrooms (too risky without expertise), white or yellow berries, plants with milky sap, umbrella-shaped flower clusters. 3) FISHING: Use improvised hooks from thorns, safety pins, or carved bone. Use worms, insects, or bright cloth as bait. 4) TRAPPING: Simple snare from wire or cord on animal trails. Figure-4 deadfall for small game. 5) COOKING: Always cook wild food when possible. Roasting over fire is simplest. Boiling is safest. WARNING: Focus energy on finding food only if you have water secured. Never eat unknown mushrooms - some are fatally toxic with no antidote.

title: Survival First Aid Kit Essentials
content: A well-prepared first aid kit can save lives in emergencies. Essential contents organized by priority: CRITICAL ITEMS: adhesive bandages (various sizes), sterile gauze pads and rolls, medical tape, antiseptic wipes or solution (povidone-iodine), antibiotic ointment, latex/nitrile gloves, CPR face shield, emergency blanket (Mylar). TOOLS: small scissors, tweezers, safety pins, instant cold pack, SAM splint or improvised splint materials, irrigation syringe for wound cleaning. MEDICATIONS: pain relievers (ibuprofen, acetaminophen), antihistamines (diphenhydramine), anti-diarrhea medication, electrolyte packets/ORS, broad-spectrum antibiotics (if prescribed). ADDITIONAL: tourniquet, wound closure strips (butterfly bandages or Steri-Strips), burn gel, eye wash solution, sunscreen, insect repellent, moleskin for blisters, hand sanitizer. IMPROVISED ALTERNATIVES when supplies run low: clean cloth strips for bandages, honey as antibacterial wound dressing, charcoal from fire for water filtration and potential toxin absorption, aloe vera for burns, tea tree oil as antiseptic, spider webs as wound dressing (helps clotting). Keep kit waterproof, check and refresh supplies every 6 months. Know how to use everything before you need it.

title: Rope and Knot Skills for Survival
content: Rope and knot skills are essential for shelter building, securing gear, climbing, and rescue. Key knots to learn: 1) BOWLINE: Creates a fixed loop that won't slip. Used for rescue, securing to trees. 'Rabbit comes out of the hole, around the tree, back in the hole.' 2) CLOVE HITCH: Quick temporary attachment to a pole or tree. Easy to adjust tension. Great for starting lashings. 3) TAUT-LINE HITCH: Adjustable loop for tent guy-lines. Slides to tighten/loosen but holds under load. 4) SQUARE KNOT (REEF KNOT): Joining two ropes of equal size. 'Right over left, left over right.' 5) FIGURE-8 KNOT: Stopper knot to prevent rope from sliding through. Foundation for the figure-8 follow-through used in climbing. 6) SHEET BEND: Joining two ropes of different sizes. More secure than square knot for unequal ropes. 7) PRUSIK KNOT: Friction hitch for ascending a rope. Slides when unloaded, grips when loaded. Making cordage from natural materials: twist plant fibers (yucca, nettle, inner bark) into two-ply cord. Technique: twist each strand clockwise while wrapping them counterclockwise around each other. Test strength before relying on improvised cordage under load.

title: Disaster Preparedness Checklist
content: Being prepared for natural and man-made disasters can save lives. Essential preparations: WATER: Store at least 4 liters per person per day for 3 days minimum (72-hour kit). Rotate supply every 6 months. Include water purification method. FOOD: Non-perishable items for 72 hours - canned goods, energy bars, dried fruit, peanut butter, crackers. Include manual can opener. DOCUMENTS: Keep copies of IDs, insurance, medical records in waterproof bag. Store digital copies on USB drive. COMMUNICATION: Battery-powered or hand-crank radio, extra phone chargers (solar charger), whistle, emergency contact list. FIRST AID: Complete first aid kit, prescription medications (7-day supply minimum), glasses/contacts. TOOLS: Flashlight with extra batteries, multi-tool, duct tape, dust masks, plastic sheeting, garbage bags. CLOTHING: Sturdy shoes, rain gear, warm layers, work gloves. SPECIAL NEEDS: Baby supplies, pet food, assistive devices. EVACUATION PLAN: Know multiple exit routes from home and area. Designate meeting points for family. Practice the plan twice yearly. SHELTER-IN-PLACE: Know how to seal a room with plastic sheeting and tape. Have enough supplies for 2 weeks if possible.

title: Compass Navigation Basics
content: A compass is the most reliable navigation tool that never needs batteries. Understanding your compass: the magnetic needle always points to magnetic north (red end). Parts: baseplate, rotating bezel with degree markings (0-360°), direction-of-travel arrow, orienting arrow, magnetic needle. Basic navigation: 1) TAKING A BEARING: Point the direction-of-travel arrow at your destination. Rotate the bezel until the orienting arrow aligns with the north end of the magnetic needle. Read the bearing at the index line. 2) FOLLOWING A BEARING: Set the desired bearing on the bezel. Hold compass flat, rotate your body until the magnetic needle aligns with the orienting arrow ('red in the shed'). Walk in the direction the direction-of-travel arrow points. 3) TRIANGULATION: Take bearings on two or three known landmarks. Draw lines on your map. Your position is where the lines intersect. Magnetic declination: the difference between magnetic north and true north varies by location. Adjust your compass or add/subtract declination from your bearings. Check declination for your area on maps or online before going offline. Common errors: holding compass near metal objects, not holding it level, confusing north and south needle ends.

title: Navigation Using Stars
content: Celestial navigation has guided travelers for thousands of years. Finding directions by stars: NORTHERN HEMISPHERE: 1) POLARIS (North Star): Find the Big Dipper (Ursa Major). The two stars at the end of the 'cup' point to Polaris. Polaris is at the end of the Little Dipper's handle. Polaris indicates true north - it barely moves as Earth rotates. 2) ORION: Orion's belt rises in the east and sets in the west. The sword points roughly south. SOUTHERN HEMISPHERE: 1) SOUTHERN CROSS (Crux): Find the four stars of the Southern Cross. Extend the long axis 4.5 times its length toward the horizon. That point is approximately celestial south. Project down to the horizon for south on the ground. 2) Using two pointer stars (Alpha and Beta Centauri): Draw a line between them, find the midpoint, draw a perpendicular line toward the Cross and extend to meet the Cross's extended line. GENERAL METHODS: 1) STICK METHOD: Plant a stick upright. Mark the tip of its shadow. Wait 15 minutes, mark the new shadow tip. A line from the first mark to the second runs roughly east-west (first mark is west in Northern Hemisphere). 2) MOON: A crescent moon's horns connected by a line extended to the horizon points roughly south (Northern Hemisphere) or north (Southern Hemisphere).

title: GPS Alternatives and Natural Navigation
content: When GPS and compass are unavailable, nature provides direction clues. SUN-BASED METHODS: 1) The sun rises roughly east and sets roughly west. At solar noon (not clock noon), the sun is due south in the Northern Hemisphere and due north in the Southern Hemisphere. 2) ANALOG WATCH METHOD: Point the hour hand at the sun. In the Northern Hemisphere, south is halfway between the hour hand and 12 o'clock. In the Southern Hemisphere, north is found by pointing 12 at the sun and finding halfway to the hour hand. 3) SHADOW STICK METHOD: Detailed above in star navigation. NATURAL INDICATORS (use multiple - none are 100% reliable alone): 1) VEGETATION: In the Northern Hemisphere, south-facing slopes get more sun and may have different vegetation. Moss prefers shade but can grow anywhere - not reliable alone. 2) WIND PATTERNS: Prevailing winds are consistent in many regions. Flagging on trees indicates dominant wind direction. 3) WATER FLOW: Rivers generally flow toward the coast. Following water downstream usually leads to settlements. 4) ANIMAL BEHAVIOR: Spider webs often face south (Northern Hemisphere). Birds often fly toward water in the morning. Ant hills may have longer slopes on the south side. 5) SNOW AND ICE: Snow melts faster on south-facing slopes (Northern Hemisphere). These natural signs should be used together and cross-referenced for accuracy.

title: Map Reading and Topographic Maps
content: Map reading is an essential skill for navigation. Key map elements: 1) LEGEND/KEY: Explains symbols used for roads, trails, water, buildings, vegetation. Always check the legend first. 2) SCALE: Ratio showing map distance to real distance (e.g., 1:50,000 means 1 cm = 500 meters). Use scale to estimate distances. 3) CONTOUR LINES: Lines connecting points of equal elevation. Closely spaced lines = steep terrain. Widely spaced = gentle slope. V-shapes pointing uphill indicate valleys (with streams). V-shapes pointing downhill indicate ridges. Closed circles indicate hilltops. 4) GRID REFERENCES: Use a 6-figure grid reference for precise locations. Read easting (left-right) first, then northing (bottom-top). 5) ORIENTATING YOUR MAP: Align your map with the terrain using a compass or by matching visible features. The top of most maps is north. Navigation techniques: 1) HANDRAIL FEATURES: Follow linear features (rivers, ridges, trails) parallel to your route. 2) CATCHING FEATURES: Identify obvious features beyond your destination that tell you if you've gone too far. 3) ATTACK POINTS: Navigate to an obvious feature near your destination, then make the final approach. 4) AIMING OFF: Deliberately aim to one side of your target so you know which way to turn when you reach the catching feature.

title: Urban Navigation and Landmark Methods
content: Navigating in cities and towns without GPS requires observation and memory. Techniques: 1) LANDMARK NAVIGATION: Identify unique, visible landmarks (tall buildings, monuments, bridges, religious structures). Note their direction relative to your position. Use them as reference points. 2) STREET PATTERNS: Many cities have grid patterns. Learn if streets run east-west or north-south. Numbered streets often increase in one direction. 3) SUN DIRECTION: The sun still indicates general direction in cities. Morning sun is east, afternoon sun is west. 4) SATELLITE DISHES: In many regions, satellite dishes point toward the equator (south in Northern Hemisphere, north in Southern). They can indicate general direction. 5) RELIGIOUS BUILDINGS: Many churches have altars facing east. Mosques have the mihrab facing Mecca. Synagogues often face Jerusalem. 6) TRANSPORTATION: Major roads usually connect to the city center. Bus and train routes converge at central hubs. Follow public transit lines to find your way to known areas. 7) MENTAL MAPPING: When exploring, create a mental map by noting turns, distances, and landmarks. Count blocks or estimate time between landmarks. 8) ASK LOCALS: People are often willing to help. Ask for directions using landmarks rather than street names. Learn basic direction words in the local language. SAFETY: Stay on well-lit, populated streets. Have your accommodation address written down. Note the nearest police station or embassy location.

title: The Scientific Method
content: The scientific method is a systematic way to learn about the world through observation and experimentation. Steps: 1) OBSERVATION: Notice something interesting or puzzling in the world around you. 2) QUESTION: Form a specific question about what you observed. Good questions are testable and measurable. Example: 'Does the amount of sunlight affect how fast plants grow?' 3) HYPOTHESIS: Make an educated guess that answers your question. A hypothesis should be testable and falsifiable. Format: 'If [I do this], then [this will happen] because [reason].' 4) EXPERIMENT: Design a test for your hypothesis. Key principles: a) Control variables - change only one thing at a time. b) Have a control group (unchanged) and experimental group (with the change). c) Repeat the experiment multiple times for reliability. d) Measure results objectively. 5) ANALYZE DATA: Collect and organize your results. Look for patterns. Use charts, graphs, and statistics. 6) CONCLUSION: Did the results support your hypothesis? Why or why not? What did you learn? 7) COMMUNICATE: Share your findings so others can verify and build on your work. Important: 'Failing' to prove a hypothesis is NOT failure - it's learning. Many great discoveries came from unexpected results. Science is a process of continuous learning and revision.

title: Basic Mathematics - Fractions, Decimals, and Percentages
content: Understanding fractions, decimals, and percentages is essential for everyday life. FRACTIONS: A fraction represents part of a whole. The top number (numerator) shows how many parts you have. The bottom number (denominator) shows how many equal parts make the whole. Example: 3/4 means 3 out of 4 equal parts. Simplifying fractions: divide both numbers by their greatest common factor. Example: 6/8 → divide both by 2 → 3/4. Adding fractions: find a common denominator. Example: 1/3 + 1/4 = 4/12 + 3/12 = 7/12. DECIMALS: Decimals represent fractions of 10. 0.1 = 1/10, 0.01 = 1/100, 0.25 = 25/100 = 1/4. Converting fraction to decimal: divide numerator by denominator. 3/4 = 3 ÷ 4 = 0.75. PERCENTAGES: 'Per cent' means 'per hundred.' 50% = 50/100 = 0.5 = 1/2. Converting decimal to percentage: multiply by 100. 0.75 × 100 = 75%. Finding a percentage of a number: 20% of 150 = 0.20 × 150 = 30. Practical applications: calculating tips (15% of meal cost), understanding discounts (30% off means you pay 70%), reading statistics (78% of students passed), measuring ingredients (3/4 cup of flour).

title: World Geography Essentials
content: Understanding world geography helps navigate, understand climate, and appreciate cultural diversity. THE EARTH: Our planet has 7 continents and 5 major oceans. Continents by size: Asia (largest), Africa, North America, South America, Antarctica, Europe, Australia/Oceania (smallest). Oceans by size: Pacific (largest), Atlantic, Indian, Southern (Antarctic), Arctic (smallest). KEY GEOGRAPHIC FEATURES: Mountains - formed by tectonic plate collisions. Major ranges: Himalayas (Asia, highest), Andes (South America, longest), Alps (Europe), Rockies (North America). Rivers - major waterways: Nile (longest, Africa), Amazon (most water volume, South America), Ganges (India), Mississippi (North America), Yangtze (China). Deserts - largest: Sahara (Africa), Arabian, Gobi (Asia), Australian Outback. CLIMATE ZONES: Tropical (near equator, hot and wet year-round), Subtropical (warm with distinct wet/dry seasons), Temperate (moderate temperatures with four seasons), Continental (extreme temperature variations), Polar (cold year-round). Time zones: Earth is divided into 24 time zones, each spanning roughly 15 degrees of longitude. The Prime Meridian (0°) passes through Greenwich, England. The International Date Line is at approximately 180° longitude in the Pacific Ocean.

title: Basic Physics - Forces and Motion
content: Understanding forces and motion helps explain how everything moves in our world. NEWTON'S THREE LAWS: 1) LAW OF INERTIA: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at the same speed and direction, unless acted upon by an unbalanced force. Example: A ball on a table stays still until you push it. You lurch forward when a car brakes because your body wants to keep moving. 2) F = ma (Force = mass × acceleration): The acceleration of an object depends on the force applied and its mass. A heavier object needs more force to accelerate. Pushing an empty cart is easier than a full one. Units: Force is measured in Newtons (N). 3) ACTION-REACTION: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. A rocket pushes gas downward, and the gas pushes the rocket upward. You push against the ground when walking, and the ground pushes you forward. KEY FORCES: Gravity - pulls objects toward Earth at 9.8 m/s². Weight = mass × gravity. Friction - resists motion between surfaces. Types: static (not moving) and kinetic (moving). Air resistance - friction with air, increases with speed and surface area. Why parachutes work. Normal force - a surface pushes back against an object resting on it. PRACTICAL APPLICATIONS: Why seatbelts save lives (inertia), why heavy trucks need longer to stop (F=ma), how airplanes fly (lift overcomes gravity).

title: Reading and Literacy Skills
content: Reading is the foundation of all learning. Strategies for improving literacy: PHONICS BASICS: Letters represent sounds. Consonants (b, c, d, f, g...) and vowels (a, e, i, o, u) combine to form words. Short vowels: a (cat), e (bed), i (sit), o (hot), u (cup). Long vowels say their name: a (cake), e (me), i (bike), o (note), u (cute). Blending: combine sounds smoothly: c-a-t → cat, sh-i-p → ship. READING COMPREHENSION STRATEGIES: 1) PREVIEW: Look at title, headings, pictures before reading. Predict what the text will be about. 2) ASK QUESTIONS: Who? What? Where? When? Why? How? 3) MAKE CONNECTIONS: Relate the text to your own experiences, other texts, or world knowledge. 4) VISUALIZE: Create mental images of what you're reading. 5) SUMMARIZE: After each section, summarize the main idea in your own words. 6) CONTEXT CLUES: Use surrounding words to figure out unknown vocabulary. WRITING BASICS: Sentences need a subject (who/what) and a verb (action). Start with capital letter, end with punctuation. Paragraphs group related sentences around one main idea. PRACTICE TIPS: Read aloud daily (even 15 minutes helps), keep a vocabulary journal, read materials you enjoy, label household objects to learn sight words, practice writing short messages daily. For adult learners: there is no shame in learning at any age. Start with materials at your comfort level and gradually increase difficulty.

title: Basic First Aid Education for Children
content: Teaching children basic first aid empowers them to help in emergencies. AGE-APPROPRIATE LESSONS: FOR AGES 5-8: 1) Know your full name, address, and a parent's phone number. 2) How to call emergency services (dial the number, state your name and location). 3) Stop, Drop, and Roll if clothing catches fire. 4) What to do if you find someone hurt (don't touch, find an adult, call for help). 5) Basic boo-boo care: wash small cuts with water, tell an adult. FOR AGES 8-12: 1) How to apply a bandage and clean a wound. 2) Recognizing emergencies (someone not breathing, severe bleeding, unconsciousness). 3) The recovery position: roll an unconscious person onto their side to keep airway clear. 4) How to use ice for bumps and sprains. 5) Recognizing allergic reactions. FOR AGES 12+: 1) Basic CPR technique. 2) Choking response (Heimlich maneuver). 3) How to use an AED (automated external defibrillator). 4) Recognizing signs of concussion. 5) How to splint a suspected fracture. TEACHING METHODS: Use role-playing and practice scenarios, create a first-aid kit together, watch age-appropriate first-aid videos, practice regularly (skills fade without practice), praise calm behavior in practice scenarios.

title: Solar System and Space Basics
content: Our solar system is a fascinating and vast neighborhood in space. THE SUN: A star at the center of our solar system. It is about 4.6 billion years old, 1.4 million km in diameter (109 times Earth's diameter), and its surface temperature is about 5,500°C. It provides light and heat essential for life on Earth. THE PLANETS (in order from the Sun): 1) MERCURY - Smallest planet, closest to Sun, extreme temperatures (-180°C to 430°C), no atmosphere, 1 year = 88 Earth days. 2) VENUS - Similar size to Earth, hottest planet (465°C due to greenhouse gases), thick toxic atmosphere, rotates backward. 3) EARTH - Our home, only known planet with life, 71% water surface, 1 moon. 4) MARS - 'Red Planet' from iron oxide, thin atmosphere, has the largest volcano (Olympus Mons), 2 small moons, target for human exploration. 5) JUPITER - Largest planet (1,321 Earths could fit inside), gas giant, Great Red Spot is a storm larger than Earth, 95 known moons. 6) SATURN - Famous for spectacular rings (made of ice and rock), gas giant, least dense planet (would float on water), 146 known moons. 7) URANUS - Ice giant, tilted on its side (98°), blue-green from methane, 27 known moons. 8) NEPTUNE - Farthest planet, ice giant, strongest winds in solar system (2,100 km/h), 16 known moons. Other objects: Dwarf planets (Pluto, Eris), asteroid belt (between Mars and Jupiter), comets, Kuiper Belt.

title: Water Cycle and Weather Basics
content: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water through Earth's systems. STAGES: 1) EVAPORATION: Heat from the Sun causes water from oceans, lakes, and rivers to turn into water vapor (gas). Warmer temperatures = more evaporation. 2) TRANSPIRATION: Plants release water vapor through their leaves. Together with evaporation, this is called evapotranspiration. 3) CONDENSATION: As water vapor rises and cools, it condenses into tiny water droplets around dust particles, forming clouds. This is why you see your breath on cold days. 4) PRECIPITATION: When water droplets in clouds combine and become heavy enough, they fall as rain, snow, sleet, or hail. Form depends on temperature. 5) COLLECTION/RUNOFF: Water flows into streams, rivers, lakes, and oceans. Some seeps into the ground (infiltration) becoming groundwater. The cycle continues. WEATHER BASICS: CLOUDS: Cumulus (fluffy, fair weather), Stratus (flat layers, overcast), Cirrus (thin, wispy, high altitude), Cumulonimbus (tall, thunderstorms). WIND: Air moves from high pressure to low pressure areas. Sea breezes blow from sea to land during the day and reverse at night. PREDICTING WEATHER WITHOUT INSTRUMENTS: Red sky at night = good weather coming. Red sky in morning = bad weather ahead. Birds flying low = storm approaching. Strong flower scents = rain likely. Leaves turning upward = windy conditions expected.

title: Diarrhea Treatment and Prevention
content: Diarrhea is one of the leading causes of death in developing countries, primarily through dehydration. Treatment: 1) ORAL REHYDRATION is the most important treatment. ORS recipe: 6 level teaspoons of sugar + half a teaspoon of salt in 1 liter of clean water. Give frequently in small sips. 2) Continue eating if possible - bananas, rice, toast, and plain crackers are gentle on the stomach (BRAT diet). 3) Avoid dairy products, fatty foods, spicy foods, and caffeine. 4) Zinc supplements reduce severity and duration in children - 20mg daily for 10-14 days (10mg for infants under 6 months). 5) Probiotics may help restore healthy gut bacteria. 6) For adults, loperamide can reduce symptoms but should NOT be used if there is bloody stool or high fever. When to seek medical help: blood in stool, fever above 38.5°C, diarrhea lasting more than 3 days in adults (or 1 day in children under 5), signs of severe dehydration, inability to keep fluids down. Prevention: wash hands with soap frequently (especially before eating and after toilet), drink only clean/purified water, cook food thoroughly, store food properly, practice good sanitation. WARNING: For infants and young children, diarrhea can become dangerous within hours due to rapid fluid loss.

title: Drowning Rescue and Water Safety
content: Drowning is a leading cause of accidental death worldwide. Recognition: A drowning person often cannot call for help - they are using all energy to breathe. Signs: head low in water with mouth at water level, head tilted back, glassy eyes, hyperventilating or gasping, trying to swim but not making progress, attempting to roll onto back, vertical body position, no leg movement. Rescue sequence: 1) REACH: Extend a pole, branch, or towel from shore. This is the safest method. 2) THROW: Throw a flotation device, rope, or any buoyant object. 3) ROW: Use a boat or board to reach the person. 4) GO: Enter the water only as a last resort if trained. Bring a flotation device. Approach from behind. After rescue: 1) Check for breathing. If not breathing, begin rescue breaths and CPR immediately. 2) Do NOT try to drain water from lungs. 3) Keep the person warm - remove wet clothes, cover with blankets. 4) Place in recovery position if breathing but unconscious. 5) All near-drowning victims should receive medical evaluation - secondary drowning (fluid in lungs) can occur hours later. Prevention: never swim alone, supervise children constantly near water, learn to swim, wear life jackets, avoid alcohol near water. WARNING: Do not attempt a swimming rescue unless you are a trained lifeguard - drowning victims can pull rescuers under.

title: Cold Weather Survival and Hypothermia
content: Hypothermia occurs when body temperature drops below 35°C (95°F). It can be fatal if untreated. Stages: MILD (35-33°C): shivering, numbness, loss of dexterity, confusion begins. MODERATE (33-28°C): violent shivering then shivering stops, severe confusion, drowsiness, slurred speech, irrational behavior (paradoxical undressing). SEVERE (below 28°C): shivering stops, loss of consciousness, weak pulse, rigid muscles, may appear dead. Treatment: 1) Get the person out of the cold and wet environment. 2) Remove wet clothing and replace with dry layers. 3) Warm the center of the body FIRST (chest, neck, head, groin) - not extremities. 4) Use skin-to-skin contact in a sleeping bag/blankets. 5) Apply warm (not hot) compresses to neck, chest, and groin. 6) Give warm beverages if conscious (NO alcohol or caffeine). 7) Handle gently - rough movement can cause cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia. 8) For severe hypothermia, seek emergency medical care. Prevention: Layer clothing (base layer wicks moisture, middle layer insulates, outer layer blocks wind/rain). Keep dry - wet clothing loses 90% of its insulating value. Eat high-calorie foods for internal heat. Stay hydrated. Recognize early symptoms and act immediately. The saying: 'Cotton kills' - it absorbs moisture and loses insulation when wet. Use wool or synthetics instead.

title: Earthquake Safety Protocol
content: Earthquakes strike without warning. Knowing what to do can save your life. DURING AN EARTHQUAKE: If INDOORS: 1) DROP to your hands and knees. 2) Take COVER under a sturdy desk, table, or against an interior wall. 3) HOLD ON to your shelter and protect your head and neck. 4) Stay inside until shaking stops. Do NOT run outside during shaking. 5) Stay away from windows, outside walls, and heavy objects that could fall. If OUTDOORS: 1) Move to an open area away from buildings, power lines, and trees. 2) Drop to the ground. 3) Protect head and neck with arms. If in a VEHICLE: 1) Pull over safely, away from bridges, overpasses, and buildings. 2) Stay inside the vehicle. 3) Set the parking brake. If near COAST: Move to higher ground immediately after shaking - a tsunami may follow. AFTER THE EARTHQUAKE: 1) Check yourself and others for injuries. 2) Expect aftershocks - they can be nearly as strong as the main quake. 3) Check for gas leaks (smell), damaged electrical wiring, structural damage. 4) Do NOT use elevators. 5) If trapped: tap on pipes or walls (don't shout - conserve energy and avoid dust inhalation). Use a whistle if available. 6) Turn on battery-powered radio for information. Prepare beforehand: secure heavy furniture, know how to shut off gas/water, keep shoes by bed (for broken glass), have emergency kit ready.

title: River Crossing Safety
content: Crossing rivers and streams safely is a critical wilderness skill. Assessment before crossing: 1) Scout upstream and downstream for the best crossing point. 2) Look for wide, shallow sections with gradual banks. Avoid narrow, deep channels with fast current. 3) Check water depth with a long stick. 4) Avoid crossings above waterfalls, rapids, or log jams. 5) Consider water temperature - cold water increases hypothermia risk. Crossing techniques: 1) SOLO CROSSING: Face upstream at a 45° angle. Use a sturdy pole (placed upstream) as a third leg. Shuffle feet - don't cross legs. Move one point of contact at a time. 2) GROUP CROSSING (Line abreast): Link arms, strongest person upstream. Move together slowly. Each person provides support for the next. 3) GROUP CROSSING (Tripod): Three people face inward in a triangle, arms on shoulders. Rotate like a wheel with the strongest person upstream. 4) ROPE ASSISTED: If available, the strongest swimmer crosses first with a rope. Others follow using the rope as a safety line, clipped with a carabiner if possible. Pack preparation: Unbuckle hip belt and sternum strap of your backpack so you can shed it if swept away. Waterproof important items in dry bags. Remove boots and wear sandals or go barefoot for better grip (carry boots across). Cross early morning when water levels are typically lowest. WARNING: Never cross a flooded river. Wait for water levels to drop - even ankle-deep floodwater can knock you down.

title: Nutrition and Healthy Eating Basics
content: Good nutrition is fundamental to health. Your body needs a balance of nutrients. MACRONUTRIENTS: 1) CARBOHYDRATES: Primary energy source. Found in grains, bread, rice, pasta, fruits, vegetables. Complex carbs (whole grains, vegetables) provide sustained energy. Simple carbs (sugar, white bread) give quick energy but fade fast. 2) PROTEINS: Build and repair muscles, organs, and immune system. Found in meat, fish, eggs, beans, lentils, nuts, dairy. Aim for variety - plant and animal sources. 3) FATS: Essential for brain function, vitamin absorption, and energy storage. Healthy fats: olive oil, nuts, avocado, fish. Limit: fried food, processed snacks, excessive butter. MICRONUTRIENTS: Vitamins (A for vision, C for immune system, D from sunlight for bones) and Minerals (Iron for blood, Calcium for bones, Zinc for immune system). HYDRATION: Drink at least 8 glasses of water daily. More during hot weather or exercise. PRACTICAL TIPS: Eat a rainbow of colorful fruits and vegetables. Fill half your plate with vegetables. Choose whole grains over refined grains. Limit added sugar and salt. Eat regular meals - don't skip breakfast. Read food labels. Cook at home when possible. For children: introduce variety early, make healthy food fun and colorful, involve children in food preparation.

title: Hygiene and Disease Prevention
content: Good hygiene practices prevent the spread of disease and save lives. HANDWASHING: The most effective disease prevention measure. Wash with soap and water for at least 20 seconds. Key times: before eating, after using toilet, after touching animals, after coughing/sneezing, before preparing food. Technique: wet hands, apply soap, rub all surfaces (palms, backs, between fingers, under nails), rinse, dry with clean cloth. If no soap available, use ash or sand as alternatives. WATER AND FOOD SAFETY: Always drink clean/purified water. Cook food to proper temperatures (meat well done in areas without refrigeration). Wash fruits and vegetables. Store food in covered containers. Keep raw and cooked foods separate. SANITATION: Use latrines or toilets - never defecate near water sources. Dispose of waste properly. Keep living areas clean and ventilated. PERSONAL HYGIENE: Bathe regularly. Brush teeth twice daily. Wear clean clothes. Trim nails. Cover mouth when coughing/sneezing. DISEASE PREVENTION: Keep vaccinations up to date. Use mosquito nets in malaria-prone areas. Wear shoes to prevent hookworm and foot infections. Avoid sharing personal items (toothbrushes, razors). MENSTRUAL HYGIENE: Use clean absorbent materials (pads, cloth, cups), change regularly, wash reusable materials with soap and dry in sunlight. Menstruation is normal - no cultural shame should prevent proper hygiene practices.
